front: What is an Entity in the ER model?
back: An entity is a real-world object or concept that exists independently and can be distinctly identified. Examples: a person, a book, a student, a bank account.

An entity type is a collection of entities that share common attributes or characteristics. It defines the structure and properties common to all entities of that type. Example: STUDENT entity type has attributes like roll_no, name, age.
An entity set is the collection or group of all entities of a particular entity type at a specific point in time. It is a set of entities of the same type that share the same attributes.

front: Differentiate between Entity Type and Entity Set
back: Entity Type is the abstract definition/blueprint (e.g., STUDENT), while Entity Set is the actual collection of entities at a given time (e.g., all students currently enrolled in a college).
A simple attribute (also called atomic attribute) cannot be divided further into smaller components. It has a single atomic value. Examples: age, salary, roll_number.
A composite attribute can be divided into smaller sub-attributes representing more basic attributes with independent meanings. Example: Address can be divided into street, city, state, zip_code.
Single-valued attributes have only one value for an entity (e.age has one value). Multi-valued attributes can have multiple values for an entity (e.g., phone_numbers, email_addresses).

A derived attribute is one whose value can be derived or calculated from other related attributes. It does not store physical data but is computed when needed. Example: age can be derived from date_of_birth.
A key attribute is an attribute or set of attributes that uniquely identifies each entity in an entity set. Its value must be unique and not null. Example: roll_number in STUDENT entity.

front: What is a Weak Entity Type?
back: A weak entity type is an entity that does not have a key attribute of its own and depends on a strong (owner) entity for identification. It is identified by combining its partial key with the primary key of the owner entity.

front: What is a Strong Entity Type?
back: A strong entity type is an entity that has its own primary key and can exist independently without depending on any other entity. It is represented by a single rectangle in ER diagram.

front: What are Structural Constraints in ER model?
back: Structural constraints define the relationship between entity types. They include Cardinality (maximum number of entities in a relationship) and Participation constraints (whether entity existence is mandatory or optional).
Cardinality specifies the number of instances of one entity that can be associated with a single instance of another entity. It can be One-to-One (1:1), One-to-Many (1:N), or Many-to-Many (M:N).
Participation constraint determines whether the existence of an entity depends on its being related to another entity through a relationship. It can be Total (mandatory) or Partial (optional).
